title: Le petit Chaperon rouge
source: Charles Perrault, « Le petit Chaperon rouge » (1697). Prose: Les Contes de Perrault, texte établi par Pierre Féron, Casterman, 1902 (p. 5-6), texte validé sur Wikisource, orthographe modernisée. Moralité en vers rétablie d’après l’original de 1697 (Histoires ou contes du temps passé, Barbin), que cette édition scolaire avait omise. Prononciation: français standard moderne.

sentences.t: Il était une fois une petite fille de village, la plus éveillée qu’on eût su voir : sa mère en était folle, et sa mère-grand plus folle encore.
sentences.l: There was once a little girl of the village, the most bright that one could have known how to see: her mother was mad about her, and her grandmother madder still.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a little village girl, the brightest you ever could have seen; her mother doted on her, and her grandmother doted still more.
sentences.n: «Il était une fois» is the fixed fairy-tale opener — literally ’it was one time,’ i.e. ’once upon a time.’ «en était folle»: «en» stands in for ’about her’ (de+elle), and «être fou de» = ’to be crazy about.’ «eût su voir» is a literary past subjunctive — the elevated storytelling register.

sentences.t: Cette bonne femme lui fit faire un petit chaperon rouge qui lui seyait si bien, que partout on l’appelait le petit Chaperon rouge.
sentences.l: This good woman had made for her a little red hood that suited her so well, that everywhere they called her the little Red Hood.
sentences.i: This good woman had a little red hood made for her that suited her so well that everyone called her Little Red Riding Hood.
sentences.n: «faire faire» = ’to have (something) made’ — French uses «faire» + infinitive for causing an action, like English ’have something done.’ «un chaperon» was a hood/cape; the girl is named for the garment. «seyait» is from the old verb «seoir» (’to suit, become’), rare today.

sentences.t: Un jour, sa mère ayant cuit et fait des galettes, lui dit : « Va voir comment se porte ta mère-grand, car on m’a dit qu’elle était malade.
sentences.l: One day, her mother having baked and made some flat-cakes, said to her: 'Go see how your grandmother is faring, for they told me that she was ill.'
sentences.i: One day, when her mother had baked some galettes, she said to her: 'Go and see how your grandmother is, for I've been told she's unwell.'
sentences.n: «ayant cuit et fait» is a present participle setting the scene (’having baked...’). «se porter» = ’to fare, to be (in health)’: «comment se porte...?» = ’how is ... doing?’ «on m’a dit»: «on» (’one/they’) for an unnamed subject, best rendered as a passive: ’I was told.’

sentences.t: Porte-lui une galette et ce petit pot de beurre. »
sentences.l: Carry to her a flat-cake and this little pot of butter.
sentences.i: Take her a galette and this little pot of butter.
sentences.n: «Porte-lui»: imperative with attached pronoun «lui» (’to her’). A «galette» is a flat round cake; «un pot de beurre», a crock of butter — the errand’s two gifts, repeated like a refrain through the tale.

sentences.t: Le petit Chaperon rouge partit aussitôt pour aller chez sa mère-grand, qui demeurait dans un autre village.
sentences.l: The little Red Hood left at once to go to (the home of) her grandmother, who dwelt in another village.
sentences.i: Little Red Riding Hood set off at once to go to her grandmother's, who lived in another village.
sentences.n: «chez» = ’at/to the home of’: «chez sa mère-grand» = ’to her grandmother’s place’ — no single English word matches it. «demeurer» = ’to dwell, reside,’ a touch more formal than everyday «habiter.»

sentences.t: En passant dans un bois, elle rencontra compère le Loup, qui eut bien envie de la manger ; mais il n’osa, à cause de quelques bûcherons qui étaient dans la forêt.
sentences.l: In passing through a wood, she met Gossip the Wolf, who had a good desire to eat her; but he did not dare, because of some woodcutters who were in the forest.
sentences.i: As she passed through a wood she met old Neighbour Wolf, who had a great mind to eat her; but he did not dare, on account of some woodcutters who were in the forest.
sentences.n: «en passant» = ’while passing’ (en + participle marks simultaneous action). «compère» is an old familiar title — ’crony, neighbour’ — applied mock-respectfully to the Wolf. «avoir envie de» = ’to feel like, want to.’ «eut», «osa» are passé simple, the literary past that carries the narration.

sentences.t: Il lui demanda où elle allait.
sentences.l: He asked her where she was going.
sentences.n: «demander à quelqu’un» = ’to ask someone’; «lui» = ’to her.’ In the indirect question French keeps the plain subject–verb order and the indicative «allait».

sentences.t: La pauvre enfant, qui ne savait pas qu’il était dangereux de s’arrêter à écouter un loup, lui dit : Je vais voir ma mère-grand, et lui porter une galette, avec un petit pot de beurre, que ma mère lui envoie.
sentences.l: The poor child, who did not know that it was dangerous to stop to listen to a wolf, said to him: 'I am going to see my grandmother, and to carry to her a flat-cake, with a little pot of butter, that my mother sends her.'
sentences.i: The poor child, who did not know that it is dangerous to stop and listen to a wolf, said to him: 'I'm going to see my grandmother, and to bring her a galette, with a little pot of butter that my mother is sending her.'
sentences.n: «il était dangereux de...»: impersonal «il» (’it’), the dummy subject of ’it is dangerous to...’ «s’arrêter à» + infinitive = ’to stop (in order) to.’ «que ma mère lui envoie»: «que» is the object relative ’that/which,’ pointing back to the pot of butter.

sentences.t: — Demeure-t-elle bien loin ? lui dit le loup.
sentences.l: 'Does she dwell very far?' said the wolf to her.
sentences.i: 'Does she live very far off?' the wolf asked her.
sentences.n: «Demeure-t-elle»: a question by inversion (verb before subject). The «-t-» is a euphonic buffer inserted only for sound, between the verb-vowel and «elle/il/on» — it means nothing. «bien» here intensifies: ’quite/very,’ not ’well.’

sentences.t: — Oh ! oui, dit le petit Chaperon rouge ; c’est par delà le moulin que vous voyez tout là-bas, à la première maison du village.
sentences.l: 'Oh! yes,' said the little Red Hood; 'it is beyond the mill that you see right over there, at the first house of the village.'
sentences.i: 'Oh yes,' said Little Red Riding Hood; 'it's past the mill you see away over there, at the first house in the village.'
sentences.n: «c’est ... que ...» is a cleft that spotlights a phrase — ’it is beyond the mill that...’ «par delà» = ’beyond, on the far side of.’ «là-bas» = ’over there, yonder’ («bas» ’low’ + «là» ’there’).

sentences.t: — Eh bien ! dit le Loup, je veux l’aller voir aussi : je m’y en vais par ce chemin-ci, et toi par ce chemin-là ; et nous verrons à qui plus tôt y sera.
sentences.l: 'Well then!' said the Wolf, 'I want to go to see her too: I go there by this path-here, and you by that path-there; and we shall see who sooner there will be.'
sentences.i: 'Well then,' said the Wolf, 'I mean to go and see her too. I'll go by this road here, and you by that road there, and we'll see which of us gets there first.'
sentences.n: «l’aller voir»: older French puts the object pronoun before the whole verb group («l’aller voir») where modern French says «aller la voir.» «je m’y en vais» stacks «y» (’there’) and «en» (’away’) onto «s’en aller» (’to go off’). «-ci» = ’this (near),’ «-là» = ’that (far).’ «à qui ... y sera» = ’who will be there first.’

sentences.t: Le Loup se mit à courir de toute sa force par le chemin qui était le plus court, et la petite fille s’en alla par le chemin le plus long, s’amusant à cueillir des noisettes, à courir après des papillons, et à faire des bouquets des petites fleurs qu’elle rencontrait.
sentences.l: The Wolf set himself to run with all his strength by the path that was the shortest, and the little girl went off by the path the longest, amusing herself in gathering hazelnuts, in running after butterflies, and in making bouquets of the little flowers that she met.
sentences.i: The Wolf set off running as fast as he could by the shortest road, while the little girl went along by the longest road, amusing herself gathering hazelnuts, chasing butterflies, and making little bouquets of the flowers she came across.
sentences.n: «se mettre à» + infinitive = ’to start to.’ «s’en aller» = ’to go off, set off.’ «s’amuser à» + infinitive = ’to enjoy doing.’ Note the trio of parallel «à + infinitive» phrases — a very French way to string ongoing activities together.

sentences.t: Le Loup ne fut pas longtemps à arriver à la maison de la mère-grand ; il heurte : toc, toc.
sentences.l: The Wolf was not long in arriving at the house of the grandmother; he knocks: knock, knock.
sentences.i: The Wolf was not long in reaching the grandmother's house; he knocks — rat-tat.
sentences.n: «ne ... pas longtemps à» = ’not long in (doing).’ Watch the tense-shift: after the passé simple, «il heurte» jumps to the present — a ’historic present’ that makes the knock vivid. «toc, toc» is the French ’knock-knock.’

sentences.t: — Qui est là ?
sentences.l: 'Who is there?'
sentences.n: «là» = ’there.’ A stock exchange you will hear at every door in the tale.

sentences.t: — C’est votre fille, le petit Chaperon rouge, dit le Loup en contrefaisant sa voix, qui vous apporte une galette et un petit pot de beurre, que ma mère vous envoie.
sentences.l: 'It is your daughter, the little Red Hood,' said the Wolf in counterfeiting his voice, 'who brings you a flat-cake and a little pot of butter, that my mother sends you.'
sentences.i: 'It's your granddaughter, Little Red Riding Hood,' said the Wolf, disguising his voice, 'bringing you a galette and a little pot of butter that my mother is sending you.'
sentences.n: «votre fille» is literally ’your daughter’ but here means the grandchild — an old affectionate usage. «en contrefaisant» = ’by counterfeiting’ (en + participle, marking manner). «contrefaire» = ’to imitate, mimic, fake.’

sentences.t: — La bonne mère-grand, qui était dans son lit, à cause qu’elle se trouvait un peu mal, lui cria : Tire la chevillette, la bobinette cherra.
sentences.l: The good grandmother, who was in her bed, because that she found herself a little ill, cried to him: 'Pull the little-peg, the little-latch will fall.'
sentences.i: The good grandmother, who was in her bed because she was feeling a little poorly, called out to him: 'Pull the peg and the latch will drop.'
sentences.n: «à cause que» = old form of «parce que» (’because’); modern French keeps only «à cause de» + noun. «se trouver mal» = ’to feel unwell.’ «chevillette» and «bobinette» are diminutives — the little peg and latch of a door-catch; «cherra» is the archaic future of «choir» (’to fall’), today «tombera». One of the tale’s famous lines.

sentences.t: — Le Loup tira la chevillette, et la porte s’ouvrit.
sentences.l: The Wolf pulled the little-peg, and the door opened (itself).
sentences.i: The Wolf pulled the peg, and the door opened.
sentences.n: «s’ouvrir» is reflexive where English is a plain intransitive: ’the door opened.’ French often uses a reflexive to say a thing happens by itself.

sentences.t: Il se jeta sur la bonne femme, et la dévora en moins de rien, car il y avait plus de trois jours qu’il n’avait mangé.
sentences.l: He threw himself upon the good woman, and devoured her in less than nothing, for there was more than three days that he had not eaten.
sentences.i: He flung himself on the good woman and devoured her in no time at all, for he had not eaten in more than three days.
sentences.n: «se jeter sur» = ’to hurl oneself at, pounce on.’ «en moins de rien» = idiom, ’in less than no time.’ «il y avait ... que ...» is the elapsed-time frame: ’it had been (three days) since...’ «n’avait mangé»: the «ne» stands alone (no «pas»), normal after this «que.» Perrault does not soften it — the grandmother is simply eaten.

sentences.t: Ensuite il ferma la porte, et s’alla coucher dans le lit de la mère-grand, en attendant le petit Chaperon rouge, qui, quelque temps après, vint heurter à la porte : toc, toc.
sentences.l: Then he shut the door, and went-himself to lie down in the bed of the grandmother, awaiting the little Red Hood, who, some time after, came to knock at the door: knock, knock.
sentences.i: Then he shut the door and went to lie down in the grandmother's bed, waiting for Little Red Riding Hood, who, some time later, came and knocked at the door — rat-tat.
sentences.n: «s’alla coucher»: again the old order, pronoun before the verb group («s’alla coucher» for modern «alla se coucher») — ’went to lie down.’ «en attendant» = ’while waiting for.’ «vint heurter»: «venir» + infinitive = ’to come (and) do.’

sentences.t: — Qui est là ?
sentences.l: 'Who is there?'

sentences.t: — Le petit Chaperon rouge, qui entendit la grosse voix du Loup, eut peur d’abord, mais, croyant que sa mère-grand était enrhumée, répondit : C’est votre fille, le petit Chaperon rouge, qui vous apporte une galette et un petit pot de beurre, que ma mère vous envoie.
sentences.l: The little Red Hood, who heard the big voice of the Wolf, had fear at first, but, believing that her grandmother was cold-stricken, answered: 'It is your daughter, the little Red Hood, who brings you a flat-cake and a little pot of butter, that my mother sends you.'
sentences.i: Little Red Riding Hood, hearing the Wolf's gruff voice, was frightened at first, but, thinking her grandmother had a cold, answered: 'It's your granddaughter, Little Red Riding Hood, bringing you a galette and a little pot of butter that my mother is sending you.'
sentences.n: «avoir peur» = ’to be afraid’ (literally ’to have fear’). «grosse voix» = ’deep, gruff voice’ («gros/grosse» = big, thick). «être enrhumé» = ’to have a cold’ (from «rhume», a head-cold) — the child’s innocent explanation for the strange voice.

sentences.t: — Le Loup lui cria en adoucissant un peu sa voix : Tire la chevillette, la bobinette cherra.
sentences.l: The Wolf cried to her in softening a little his voice: 'Pull the little-peg, the little-latch will fall.'
sentences.i: The Wolf called out to her, softening his voice a little: 'Pull the peg and the latch will drop.'
sentences.n: «en adoucissant» = ’by softening’ (en + participle). «adoucir» = ’to soften, sweeten,’ from «doux» (’soft, sweet’) — the same root returns in the moral’s «doucereux.» The peg-and-latch rhyme repeats, now aimed at the child.

sentences.t: — Le petit Chaperon rouge tira la chevillette, et la porte s’ouvrit.
sentences.l: The little Red Hood pulled the little-peg, and the door opened (itself).
sentences.i: Little Red Riding Hood pulled the peg, and the door opened.

sentences.t: Le Loup, la voyant entrer, lui dit en se cachant dans le lit, sous la couverture : Mets la galette et le petit pot de beurre sur la huche, et viens te coucher avec moi.
sentences.l: The Wolf, seeing her come in, said to her in hiding himself in the bed, under the cover: 'Put the flat-cake and the little pot of butter on the bread-bin, and come lie down with me.'
sentences.i: The Wolf, seeing her come in, said to her as he hid himself in the bed under the covers: 'Put the galette and the little pot of butter on the bread-bin, and come and lie down with me.'
sentences.n: «la voyant entrer»: «voir» + infinitive = ’to see (someone) do’ — ’seeing her enter.’ «se cacher» = ’to hide (oneself).’ «la huche» is an old kneading-trough / bread-bin. «viens te coucher» = imperative ’come lie down’ («se coucher» reflexive, ’to go to bed’).

sentences.t: Le petit Chaperon rouge se déshabille, et va se mettre dans le lit, où elle fut bien étonnée de voir comment sa mère-grand était faite en son déshabillé.
sentences.l: The little Red Hood undresses herself, and goes to put herself in the bed, where she was quite astonished to see how her grandmother was made in her undress.
sentences.i: Little Red Riding Hood undresses and goes to get into the bed, where she was quite astonished to see how her grandmother looked in her nightclothes.
sentences.n: «se déshabille» (present again, for immediacy) — «se déshabiller», ’to undress.’ «aller se mettre» = ’to go and put oneself,’ i.e. ’go and get into.’ «en son déshabillé»: «un déshabillé» (noun from the same verb) is a state of undress / nightdress. Perrault keeps the scene as it is: the girl gets into bed with the disguised Wolf.

sentences.t: — Elle lui dit : Ma mère-grand, que vous avez de grands bras !
sentences.l: She said to him: 'My grandmother, what you have big arms!'
sentences.i: She said to him: 'Grandmother, what big arms you have!'
sentences.n: «que vous avez de grands bras !» is the exclamative «que...» = ’how...! / what a lot of...!’ — ’what big arms you have!’ After such a quantity word French uses plain «de» (not «des») before adjective + noun: «de grands bras.» The child uses respectful «vous» to her grandmother. First of the famous rising exchanges.

sentences.t: — C’est pour mieux t’embrasser, ma fille !
sentences.l: 'It is for better to embrace you, my daughter!'
sentences.i: 'It's the better to hug you, my child!'
sentences.n: «pour mieux» + infinitive = ’in order to ... better’ — the refrain of every reply (’all the better to ... you’). «t’embrasser»: the Wolf drops to the intimate «tu» («t’» = ’you’). «embrasser» here is the older ’to clasp in the arms’; in modern French it usually means ’to kiss.’

sentences.t: — Ma mère-grand, que vous avez de grandes jambes !
sentences.l: 'My grandmother, what you have big legs!'
sentences.i: 'Grandmother, what big legs you have!'

sentences.t: — C’est pour mieux courir, mon enfant !
sentences.l: 'It is for better to run, my child!'
sentences.i: 'It's the better to run, my child!'

sentences.t: — Ma mère-grand, que vous avez de grandes oreilles !
sentences.l: 'My grandmother, what you have big ears!'
sentences.i: 'Grandmother, what big ears you have!'

sentences.t: — C’est pour mieux écouter, mon enfant !
sentences.l: 'It is for better to listen, my child!'
sentences.i: 'It's the better to hear, my child!'

sentences.t: — Ma mère-grand, que vous avez de grands yeux !
sentences.l: 'My grandmother, what you have big eyes!'
sentences.i: 'Grandmother, what big eyes you have!'

sentences.t: — C’est pour mieux te voir, mon enfant !
sentences.l: 'It is for better to see you, my child!'
sentences.i: 'It's the better to see you, my child!'
sentences.n: By now the Wolf’s «vous» has fully slipped to «te/tu» — the false-grandmother mask is giving way to the predator beneath, one line before the end.

sentences.t: — Ma mère-grand, que vous avez de grandes dents !
sentences.l: 'My grandmother, what you have big teeth!'
sentences.i: 'Grandmother, what big teeth you have!'

sentences.t: — C’est pour te manger !
sentences.l: 'It is for to eat you!'
sentences.i: 'It's to eat you up!'
sentences.n: The refrain breaks: this time there is no «mieux» (’better’) — just «pour te manger», ’to eat you.’ Perrault cuts the pattern short, and the Wolf devours her. There is no rescuing huntsman in Perrault: the tale ends here, and the moral follows.

sentences.t: Et, en disant ces mots, ce méchant Loup se jeta sur le petit Chaperon rouge, et la mangea.
sentences.l: And, in saying these words, this wicked Wolf threw himself upon the little Red Hood, and ate her.
sentences.i: And, saying these words, the wicked Wolf flung himself upon Little Red Riding Hood and ate her up.
sentences.n: «en disant» = ’while/on saying’ (en + participle once more). «méchant» = ’wicked, nasty.’ «la mangea» (passé simple of «manger») closes the story bluntly.

sentences.t:
On voit ici que de jeunes enfants,
Surtout de jeunes filles
Belles, bien faites, et gentilles,
Font très mal d’écouter toute sorte de gens,
Et que ce n’est pas chose étrange,
S’il en est tant que le Loup mange.
Je dis le Loup, car tous les Loups
Ne sont pas de la même sorte ;
Il en est d’une humeur accorte,
Sans bruit, sans fiel et sans courroux,
Qui privés, complaisants et doux,
Suivent les jeunes Demoiselles
Jusque dans les maisons, jusque dans les ruelles ;
Mais hélas ! qui ne sait que ces Loups doucereux,
De tous les Loups sont les plus dangereux.
sentences.l:
One sees here that young children,
above all young girls,
beautiful, well-made, and pretty,
do very wrong to listen to every sort of person,
and that it is not a strange thing
if so many of them the Wolf eats.
I say the Wolf, for all Wolves
are not of the same sort;
there are some of an obliging humour,
without noise, without gall and without wrath,
who, tame, complaisant and sweet,
follow the young Damsels
even into the houses, even into the alleys;
but alas! who does not know that these honeyed Wolves,
of all the Wolves are the most dangerous.
sentences.i:
Here we see that young children,
young girls above all —
lovely, shapely, and sweet —
do very wrong to heed just anyone;
and it is no strange thing
if so many of them the Wolf devours.
I say the Wolf, for not all wolves
are of the selfsame kind:
some are of an obliging temper —
no noise, no bitterness, no rage —
who, tame, complaisant and mild,
follow the young ladies
right into their homes, right into the lanes;
but alas! who does not know that these honey-tongued wolves
are, of all wolves, the most dangerous of all.
sentences.n: Perrault’s verse «moralité» makes the allegory explicit: the real ’wolves’ are smooth, charming men who pursue young women — «doucereux» (’honey-tongued, saccharine,’ from «doux»). «une humeur accorte» = ’an obliging, gracious disposition.’ «privés» here means ’tame’ (as of a tamed animal), not ’deprived.’ «ruelles» is literally ’little streets/alleys,’ but in Perrault’s day also the space beside a lady’s bed where she received guests — a pointed double meaning. This school edition dropped the moral; it is restored here from Perrault’s 1697 original.

glossary.a: avoir — has (3sg present of avoir 'to have'; auxiliary in on m'a dit)
glossary.adoucissant: adoucir — softening (present participle of adoucir 'to soften')
glossary.alla: aller — went (3sg passé simple; s'en alla 'went off')
glossary.allait: aller — was going (3sg imperfect of aller)
glossary.appelait: appeler — called (3sg imperfect; on l'appelait 'they called her')
glossary.apporte: apporter — brings (3sg present of apporter 'to bring')
glossary.après: après — after (prep./adv.; courir après 'to run after')
glossary.arrêter: arrêter — to stop (infinitive; here reflexive s'arrêter 'to stop oneself')
glossary.attendant: attendre — waiting (present participle; en attendant 'while waiting for')
glossary.avait: avoir — had (3sg imperfect; also il y avait 'there was')
glossary.avez: avoir — you have (2pl present of avoir)
glossary.bas: bas — low (adj./adv.; here in là-bas 'over there')
glossary.bien: bien — well; quite, indeed (adverb; eh bien 'well then')
glossary.bobinette: bobinette — little latch (noun, f.; diminutive, the latch of a door-catch)
glossary.bonne: bon — good (feminine of bon; bonne femme 'good woman')
glossary.bras: bras — arm(s) (noun, m.; same form sg. and pl.)
glossary.c: ce — it, that (elided ce in c'est 'it is')
glossary.cachant: cacher — hiding (present participle; se cachant 'hiding oneself')
glossary.cause: cause — cause (noun, f.; à cause de/que 'because of/that')
glossary.ce: ce — this, that; it (demonstrative adjective / pronoun)
glossary.chaperon: chaperon — hood (noun, m.; the girl's red hood, hence her name)
glossary.cherra: choir — will fall (3sg future of the archaic choir 'to fall'; modern tombera)
glossary.chevillette: chevillette — little peg, pin (noun, f.; diminutive, the peg of a door-latch)
glossary.ci: ci — here (adverbial particle; chemin-ci 'this path here')
glossary.compère: compère — crony, neighbour, gossip (noun, m.; old familiar title)
glossary.contrefaisant: contrefaire — imitating, faking (present participle of contrefaire 'to counterfeit')
glossary.coucher: coucher — to lie down, put to bed (infinitive; here reflexive se coucher)
glossary.cria: crier — cried, called out (3sg passé simple of crier)
glossary.croyant: croire — believing, thinking (present participle of croire)
glossary.cuit: cuire — baked, cooked (past participle of cuire 'to cook, bake')
glossary.d: de — of, from (elided de before a vowel: d'abord, d'écouter, d'une)
glossary.delà: delà — beyond (in par delà 'beyond, on the far side of')
glossary.demanda: demander — asked (3sg passé simple of demander)
glossary.demeurait: demeurer — dwelt, lived (3sg imperfect of demeurer 'to dwell')
glossary.demeure: demeurer — dwells, lives (3sg present of demeurer)
glossary.demoiselles: demoiselle — young ladies, damsels (noun, f. plural)
glossary.des: de — of the; some (partitive; contraction of de + les)
glossary.dis: dire — I say (1sg present of dire)
glossary.disant: dire — saying (present participle; en disant 'while saying')
glossary.dit: dire — said; says (3sg passé simple / present of dire)
glossary.doucereux: doucereux — honeyed, sugary-sweet, smooth (adjective, m.; from doux)
glossary.du: de — of the (contraction of de + le)
glossary.déshabille: déshabiller — undresses (3sg present; se déshabille 'undresses herself')
glossary.déshabillé: déshabillé — state of undress, nightclothes (noun, m.; from déshabiller)
glossary.dévora: dévorer — devoured (3sg passé simple of dévorer)
glossary.eh: eh — well!, hey! (interjection; eh bien 'well then')
glossary.elle: elle — she, her (subject / stressed pronoun, f.)
glossary.embrasser: embrasser — to embrace, hug (infinitive; older sense 'to clasp in the arms')
glossary.en: en — in; while; of it/them (preposition and pronoun)
glossary.enrhumée: enrhumer — having a cold (past participle, f.; être enrhumé 'to have a cold')
glossary.entendit: entendre — heard (3sg passé simple of entendre 'to hear')
glossary.envie: envie — desire, longing (noun, f.; avoir envie de 'to want to')
glossary.eut: avoir — had (3sg passé simple of avoir)
glossary.eût: avoir — (that one) might have (3sg past subjunctive of avoir; literary)
glossary.faire: faire — to make, do (infinitive; also faire faire 'to have made')
glossary.fait: faire — made, done (past participle / 3sg present of faire)
glossary.faite: faire — made, formed (past participle, f.; était faite 'was formed, looked')
glossary.faites: faire — made, shapely (past participle, f. plural; here 'well-made')
glossary.femme: femme — woman (noun, f.; bonne femme 'good woman')
glossary.ferma: fermer — shut, closed (3sg passé simple of fermer)
glossary.fille: fille — daughter, girl (noun, f.; votre fille = granddaughter here)
glossary.fit: faire — made, had (done) (3sg passé simple; fit faire 'had made')
glossary.fois: fois — time, occasion (noun, f.; il était une fois 'once upon a time')
glossary.folle: fou — mad, crazy (feminine of fou; être folle de 'mad about')
glossary.font: faire — do, make (3pl present of faire; font mal 'do wrong')
glossary.force: force — strength, force (noun, f.; de toute sa force 'with all his might')
glossary.fut: être — was (3sg passé simple of être)
glossary.gentilles: gentil — nice, sweet, pretty (feminine plural of gentil)
glossary.grand: grand — big, great (adjective, m.; in mère-grand 'grandmother')
glossary.grosse: gros — big, deep, thick (feminine of gros; grosse voix 'deep voice')
glossary.heurte: heurter — knocks (3sg present of heurter 'to knock, strike')
glossary.il: il — he, it (subject pronoun, m.; also impersonal 'it')
glossary.jeta: jeter — threw (3sg passé simple; se jeta sur 'flung himself on')
glossary.jusque: jusque — even to, right up to (preposition)
glossary.l: le / la — the; him/her, it (elided article or object pronoun before a vowel: l'appelait, l'aller)
glossary.la: la — the; her, it (feminine article / object pronoun)
glossary.le: le — the; him, it (masculine article / object pronoun)
glossary.lui: lui — to him, to her; him (indirect / stressed pronoun)
glossary.là: là — there (adverb; also chemin-là 'that path there')
glossary.m: me — me, to me (elided me in m'a 'to me', m'y)
glossary.mal: mal — badly, ill; wrong (adv./noun; un peu mal 'unwell', font mal 'do wrong')
glossary.mangea: manger — ate (3sg passé simple of manger)
glossary.mets: mettre — put! (2sg imperative of mettre 'to put')
glossary.mettre: mettre — to put; se mettre 'to put oneself' (infinitive)
glossary.mieux: mieux — better (adverb; pour mieux 'in order to ... better')
glossary.mit: mettre — set, put (3sg passé simple; se mit à 'began to')
glossary.moins: moins — less (adverb; en moins de rien 'in no time')
glossary.même: même — same (adjective; la même sorte 'the same kind')
glossary.n: ne — not (elided ne in n'osa, n'avait — the negative particle)
glossary.ne: ne — not (negative particle, pairs with pas)
glossary.osa: oser — dared (3sg passé simple of oser 'to dare')
glossary.ouvrit: ouvrir — opened (3sg passé simple; s'ouvrit 'opened', intransitive)
glossary.partit: partir — set off, left (3sg passé simple of partir)
glossary.passant: passer — passing (present participle; en passant 'while passing')
glossary.pauvre: pauvre — poor (adjective; before the noun = 'poor, to be pitied')
glossary.peur: peur — fear (noun, f.; avoir peur 'to be afraid')
glossary.plus: plus — more (adverb; le plus 'the most', plus tôt 'sooner')
glossary.porte: porte / porter — door (noun, f.); or 'carry!' (imperative of porter, as in Porte-lui)
glossary.privés: privé — tame, domesticated (adjective, m. plural; here 'tamed', not 'deprived')
glossary.qu: que — that, which; whom; than (elided que before a vowel: qu'on, qu'elle, qu'il)
glossary.que: que — that, which, whom; than; how! (conjunction / relative / exclamative)
glossary.qui: qui — who, which, that (relative / interrogative pronoun)
glossary.rencontra: rencontrer — met (3sg passé simple of rencontrer 'to meet')
glossary.rencontrait: rencontrer — met, came across (3sg imperfect of rencontrer)
glossary.rien: rien — nothing (pronoun; en moins de rien 'in no time')
glossary.ruelles: ruelle — lanes, alleys (noun, f. plural; also the bedside space where ladies received guests)
glossary.répondit: répondre — answered, replied (3sg passé simple of répondre)
glossary.s: se / si — himself/herself; if (elided se in s'arrêter, s'en, s'alla, s'ouvrit; or si in s'il 'if')
glossary.sait: savoir — knows (3sg present of savoir 'to know')
glossary.se: se — himself, herself, itself, oneself (reflexive pronoun)
glossary.sera: être — will be (3sg future of être; y sera 'will be there')
glossary.seyait: seoir — suited, became (3sg imperfect of the old verb seoir 'to suit')
glossary.sorte: sorte — sort, kind (noun, f.; toute sorte de 'all sorts of')
glossary.su: savoir — known (past participle of savoir; eût su voir 'could have seen how to see')
glossary.suivent: suivre — follow (3pl present of suivre 'to follow')
glossary.t: te / tu — you; or euphonic -t- (elided te in t'embrasser; or the meaningless linking -t- in demeure-t-elle)
glossary.te: te — you, to you (object pronoun, familiar)
glossary.tira: tirer — pulled (3sg passé simple of tirer 'to pull')
glossary.tout: tout — all; quite, right (adj./adverb; tout là-bas 'right over there')
glossary.toute: tout — all, every (feminine; toute sorte 'every sort')
glossary.trouvait: trouver — found (3sg imperfect; se trouvait mal 'was feeling unwell')
glossary.va: aller — goes; go! (3sg present / imperative of aller)
glossary.vais: aller — I go, am going (1sg present of aller)
glossary.verrons: voir — (we) shall see (1pl future of voir)
glossary.veux: vouloir — (I) want (1sg present of vouloir 'to want')
glossary.viens: venir — come! (2sg imperative of venir 'to come')
glossary.vint: venir — came (3sg passé simple of venir)
glossary.voit: voir — sees (3sg present; on voit 'one sees')
glossary.vous: vous — you (formal / plural pronoun; also 'to you')
glossary.voyez: voir — you see (2pl present of voir)
glossary.yeux: œil — eyes (noun, m. plural; irregular plural of œil 'eye')
glossary.étonnée: étonner — astonished (past participle, f.; être étonné 'to be astonished')
glossary.éveillée: éveillé — bright, alert, lively (adjective, f.; from éveiller 'to awaken')
